main_sections.title: Harvest Hymn
main_sections.poem_text:
Men's Voices:
Lord of the lotus, lord of the harvest,
Bright and munificent lord of the morn!
Thine is the bounty that prospered our sowing,
Thine is the bounty that nurtured our corn.
We bring thee our songs and our garlands for tribute,
The gold of our fields and the gold of our fruit;
O giver of mellowing radiance, we hail thee,
We praise thee, O Surya, with cymbal and flute.

Lord of the rainbow, lord of the harvest,
Great and beneficent lord of the main!
Thine is the mercy that cherished our furrows,
Thine is the mercy that fostered our grain.
We bring thee our thanks and our garlands for tribute,
The wealth of our valleys, new-garnered and ripe;
O sender of rain and the dewfall, we hail thee,
We praise thee, Varuna, with cymbal and pipe.

Women's Voices:
Queen of the gourd-flower, queen of the harvest,
Sweet and omnipotent mother, O Earth!
Thine is the plentiful bosom that feeds us,
Thine is the womb where our riches have birth.
We bring thee our love and our garlands for tribute,
With gifts of thy opulent giving we come;
O source of our manifold gladness, we hail thee,
We praise thee, O Prithvi, with cymbal and drum.

All Voices:
Lord of the Universe, Lord of our being,
Father eternal, ineffable Om!
Thou art the Seed and the Scythe of our harvests,
Thou art our Hands and our Heart and our Home.
We bring thee our lives and our labours for tribute,
Grant us thy succour, thy counsel, thy care.
O Life of all life and all blessing, we hail thee,
We praise thee, O Bramha, with cymbal and prayer.
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Harvest Hymn by Sarojini Naidu is a devotional ode of gratitude offered by the farming community at harvest time. Structured as four stanzas sung by distinct voices — men, women, and all together — the hymn addresses three elemental deities: Surya (the Sun), Varuna (the Rain), and Prithvi (the Earth), before culminating in an address to Brahma, the universal creator. The central theme is the deep, reverent interdependence between farmers and the forces of nature that sustain life. The tone is celebratory and humble simultaneously — the speakers bring songs, garlands, the literal gold of their fields, and ultimately their own lives as tribute. Naidu employs rich imagery of light, moisture, seed, and harvest; anaphora ('Thine is... Thine is') creates a litany-like rhythm that reinforces collective devotion. The dominant metaphor in the final stanza — God as both Seed and Scythe — encapsulates the Hindu concept of the divine as the source and end of all creation. Personification of Earth as an all-nourishing mother is especially vivid. The poem's musical quality, achieved through repeated refrains ('we hail thee... we praise thee') and the mention of cymbals, flute, pipe, drum, and prayer, recreates the sensory atmosphere of a harvest festival.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Write the stanza number for the main idea of each stanza given below. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: The speakers recognise the lord's kindness and praise him for nourishing the field.
The speakers praise the generosity of the creator of the universe who ensures a rich harvest.
The speakers devote their lives and work as a mark of respect to the lord and seek his support, wisdom, and care.
The speakers pay homage to the Earth and appreciate her role in supporting life, by dedicating their harvest.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks by choosing the appropriate words or phrases from within the brackets.

question_bank.stem: The setting of the poem is a rural landscape, possibly during a __________________ (harvest/religious) festival.
The tone of the poem is respectful and celebratory because it praises ___________________ (the men and women / nature and the Lord) with deep respect and gratitude.

In the line 'Thou art our Hands and our Heart and our Home', the letter 'h' is capitalised in 'Hands', 'Heart' and 'Home' because ___________________. (it represents the hard work of farmers / God resides in them)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Identify the poetic device used in the following lines from the poem.

question_bank.stem: Bright and munificent lord of the morn!
We bring thee our lives and our labours for tribute

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Match the lines from the poem in Column 1 with their expressions in Column 2. Choose the appropriate poetic device from the box given below and write in Column 3. Devices: imagery, alliteration, simile, metaphor, personification.

question_bank.stem: Match: 'sweet and omnipotent mother' — (i) creates a clear description of the scene and appeals to the senses / (ii) implied comparison of growth and reaping with beginning and end of all life / (iii) the earth is being given human-like qualities
question_bank.table:
Line|Expression|Poetic Device
sweet and omnipotent mother|(i) creates a clear description of the scene and appeals to the senses|
The wealth of our valleys, new-garnered and ripe|(ii) implied comparison of growth and reaping with beginning and end of all life|
Thou art the Seed and the Scythe|(iii) the earth is being given human-like qualities|

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Certain words or phrases are repeated in the same line of the poem. For example, The gold of our fields and the gold of our fruit. Find more such repetitions from the poem. What is the poet's purpose of using repetition in the poem?
The poem uses symbolism to convey deeper meanings. Match the symbols in Column 1 with the meanings they convey in Column 2.

question_bank.stem: Match symbols to meanings: Prithvi (the Earth), Surya (the Sun), Varuna (Rain) with their symbolic meanings.
question_bank.table:
Symbol|Meaning
Prithvi (the Earth)|(i) light, energy, life—representing the giver of warmth and prosperity
Surya (the Sun)|(ii) mercy and nourishment—crucial for agriculture and sustenance
Varuna (Rain)|(iii) fertile land, motherhood, nurturing—the source of all life

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 1: We bring thee our songs and our garlands for tribute, / The gold of our fields and the gold of our fruit; / O giver of mellowing radiance, we hail thee, / We praise thee, O Surya, with cymbal and flute.

question_bank.stem: What does the act of bringing songs and garlands as tribute suggest about the people's relationship with the divine? A. They fear the gods and wish to calm them in any way possible. B. They have a deep sense of reverence and gratitude towards the divine. C. They are forced to perform rituals without understanding the divine. D. They depend on the gods for their daily needs and activities.
options: They fear the gods and wish to calm them in any way possible.
They have a deep sense of reverence and gratitude towards the divine.
They are forced to perform rituals without understanding the divine.
They depend on the gods for their daily needs and activities.

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence suitably. The word 'gold' in the phrases 'the gold of our fields' and 'the gold of our fruit' symbolises ________________.
How does the description 'O giver of mellowing radiance' help us understand Surya's role in the harvest?
Fill in the blank by selecting the correct option. The use of cymbal and flute signifies ________________. (joy and celebration / meditation and worship)

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extract and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 2: All Voices: / Lord of the Universe, Lord of our being, / Father eternal, ineffable Om! / Thou art the Seed and the Scythe of our harvests, / Thou art our Hands and our Heart and our Home.

question_bank.stem: In the phrase 'Lord of our being,' what does the word 'being' most likely refer to? A. Our physical body B. Our entire existence C. Our spiritual practices D. Our earthly possessions

Identify whether the following statement is true or false. The imagery of 'the Seed and the Scythe' symbolises the divine's role as both the beginning (creation) and the end (harvest) of the agricultural cycle.
Identify the phrases from the extract that mean the following: A. The divine as a timeless, everlasting presence B. Beyond human comprehension or description
How do we know that these lines from the extract are spoken as a chorus?
Why do the farmers hail the God of rain?
What is being referred to as 'wealth of our valleys'? Why?
State one reason why the women refer to the Earth as a 'source of manifold gladness'.
Why does everyone in the poem offer their 'labours for tribute'?
Why has the poet presented the poem as voices of men, women, and all voices?
How does the poet create the joyous atmosphere of rural India at harvesting time?
Why is the poem presented as a hymn?

listening.transcript_text: Hello everyone! Did you know that rice is actually a type of grass, and the part we eat is its seed? Let me tell you about the process of cultivating rice. It all starts with preparing the land. First, the soil has to be ploughed. Rice is typically grown in fertile soil that holds a lot of water. The most important step in preparing the soil is to soak it with plenty of water. This step is called puddling. Once the nursery bed is prepared, the seeds can be planted. After that, we sow the seeds on the soil's surface. Paddy farmers have to ensure that their fields stay flooded with water because irrigation is crucial. It takes around 4 to 5 weeks for the seeds to grow into seedlings. After this comes the most important step—transplanting. The seedlings that were prepared in the nursery bed are uprooted and then transplanted into the flooded field. In around 120 days, the rice plants are fully grown. At this point, the crops are harvested by cutting them. Following this, the seeds are separated from the plant. This is called threshing. To reduce the moisture content even more, the separated grains are dried. This ensures that they can be stored or processed further. Through a process called milling, the rice is separated from the outer covering on it. Finally, the rice reaches the market, and that's where we get it to make all kinds of delicious foods. Whether it's Appam, Pulao, Khichdi, or Daal Bhaat, rice is a big part of many meals.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Listen to the talk about paddy cultivation. As you listen, number the events in the correct order in which they happen.

question_bank.stem: Transplanting of seedlings
Ploughing of soil
Threshing of grains
Puddling of land
Harvesting of paddy
Irrigating the field
Milling of rice

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now, listen to the talk again. As you listen, choose the correct option to answer the questions that follow.

question_bank.stem: What is the main step in preparing the soil for cultivation of rice? (i) Ploughing the soil (ii) Clearing the soil (iii) Puddling the soil
How long does it take for rice seeds to grow into seedlings before being transplanted? (i) 2–3 weeks (ii) 4–5 weeks (iii) 6–7 weeks
Why is drying rice grains important after threshing? (i) To get rice seeds for plantation (ii) To separate the rice grains easily (iii) To store the rice grains for longer
options: To get rice seeds for plantation
To separate the rice grains easily
To store the rice grains for longer

tasks_verbatim.task_text: In English, some letters are not pronounced in certain words. The letter 'n' is silent when it appears with 'm' at the end of the word: hymn, column, autumn, solemn. The letter 'b' is silent when it appears with 'm': womb, tomb, bomb, climb, comb. Now, say these words aloud.

Work in groups and make a presentation on any popular dish made during the harvest festival in your region. Include: What is the dish called? Is it known by any other name in other parts of India? What ingredients are used? How is the dish prepared? How is it served? What does the dish taste like? What is the special ritual related to the preparation? Describe any special memory associated with the preparation.

question_bank.stem: Prepare and deliver a group presentation on a popular harvest festival dish from your region using the given prompts.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Your school wishes to express gratitude to the farmers who play an important role in providing us the food on our plates. Draft a creative slogan to be put up on the school notice board. Remember to: focus on the main idea to be expressed, use simple language, be concise—express yourself in a few words. You may write the slogan on a chart paper and decorate it.

question_bank.stem: Draft a creative slogan expressing gratitude to farmers, to be displayed on the school notice board.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Match the pictures of the farming implements given in Column 1 with their use given in Column 2. Then, fill in the blanks in Column 3 to complete the name of the implement.

question_bank.stem: Match farming implement pictures with their uses and complete names: S__CKL__, SC__TH__, PL__GH, TR__W__L, R__K__, SP__D__
question_bank.table:
Implement Image|Use|Incomplete Name
1|(i) A tool with a long handle with metal teeth for collecting hay or leaves.|S__CKL__
2|(ii) A small hand tool used for digging and making shallow furrows.|SC__TH__
3|(iii) A hand-held tool with a curved blade attached to a short handle. Used for harvesting crops.|PL__GH
4|(iv) A tool pulled by oxen. Used for turning over the soil in the field so that seeds can be sown or planted.|TR__W__L
5|(v) A farm tool having a flat rectangular blade attached to a long wooden handle used for digging the soil.|R__K__
6|(vi) A hand-held tool with a long-curved blade attached to a long handle used for cutting tall grass.|SP__D__

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Choose the correct synonyms for the underlined words in the given sentences from the box below. Circle the words or phrases in the sentences that helped you to infer the meanings. Box: generous, help, brightness, indescribable, kind.

question_bank.stem: The king was beneficent to the poor because he took good care of them. synonym: kind (example done)
He was munificent towards the needy and donated a lot for their health care. synonym: ____________________
When he lost his job, all his friends gave him succour by supporting him with money. synonym: ____________________
When the sun's rays came into the dark room, the whole room was full of radiance. synonym: ____________________
The little girl was speechless with ineffable joy when she got her birthday gift from her aunt. synonym: ____________________

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You have learnt about some archaic words. Some more archaic words are given below. Refer to a dictionary to find commonly used words which are similar in meaning. 1. morrow 2. hither 3. quoth 4. betwixt 5. yonder

question_bank.stem: Find modern equivalents for: morrow, hither, quoth, betwixt, yonder

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Millets are a varied group of small-seeded grasses. They have more fibre and minerals than other cereals. Read the information on Pearl Millet given below. Work in groups of four and do the following activities: (i) Find out if millets are a part of your cuisine. (ii) Find recipes with millets and compile them into a booklet. (iii) Create a recipe of your choice.

question_bank.stem: Find out if millets are a part of your cuisine.
Find recipes with millets and compile them into a booklet.
Create a recipe of your choice using millets.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Songs related to harvest season form a part of our culture and heritage. Talk to the elders in your house and learn a song sung on a harvest festival. Share it with your classmates and teacher. Then, compile all the songs to create a songbook with the help of teacher.

question_bank.stem: Learn a harvest festival song from elders and contribute to a class songbook.
